title: Global urban visual perception varies across demographics and personalities

data_scope.source_imagery: NUS Global Streetscapes (~10M street-level images across 688 cities; raw from Mapillary and KartaView)
data_scope.exact_rated_image_count: not stated in abstract (specified in Methods)

core_conclusion: Urban visual perception is not universal: ratings of how safe, lively, wealthy, beautiful, boring or depressing a streetscape looks, plus four new behaviour-linked indicators (live nearby, walk, cycle, green), vary systematically with the viewer's gender, age, income, education, race/ethnicity and personality, and with location-based sentiment. ML models trained on existing pooled global perception datasets are biased, over-predicting positive indicators and under-predicting negative ones versus balanced human responses, showing local context is essential.
why_it_matters: Exposes a hidden bias in urban-perception research (pooling multi-cultural ratings erases demographic difference), audits the ML perception models planners increasingly use and finds systematic optimism bias, extends perception to four behaviour-relevant indicators, and releases an open demographically balanced dataset (SPECS) for fairness-aware re-training.
why_nature: Global yet human-centred street-view science that foregrounds who is looking; genuinely cross-disciplinary (geography, cultural studies, psychology/personality); a methodological warning of broad reach (widely used perception models are biased); and a durable open data + code community resource.
added_value_to_my_research: Demographic-conditioned debiasing layer -> fairness audit recipe for my explainable GeoAI spatial predictors
Perceived walk/cycle/live-nearby vs measured proximity -> demographic distance-decay weighting in 2SFCA/R2SFCA
Perceived-green vs segmented green-view index -> perception-aware environmental exposure metrics via data fusion
Perceived safety vs surveillance and racial context -> mediator analysis for space-human health-policy coupling
Spatial representation-bias audit of crowd-sourced SVI -> spatial-entropy FAIR protocol before foundation-model training
